html:
Sun Max Tech is not a touch-panel or cover-glass turnaround. It is a small DC brushless cooling fan and thermal-solutions maker, listed in Taiwan as 動力-KY, with products across GPU and PC fans, notebook fans, LED/home appliance fans, industrial control, netcom, automotive, and server applications. The old draft that treated it as a display-chain casualty was simply wrong. The more bullish draft had the right direction, cooling, but overreached by making it sound like a near-pure AI server supplier. The actual opportunity is subtler and more interesting: a small, retail-owned Taiwan thermal component company that has already proven high-margin earnings power in the RTX/gaming upgrade cycle, has optionality in server and automotive cooling, and is now being sold as if one weak shipment window means the cycle is over.

At the May 18, 2026 close, 6591 trades at TWD 52.50, with about 41.1 million shares, a TWD 2.16 billion market cap, 1.0x book value, TWD 52.38 book per share, and near-four-quarter EPS of TWD 4.43 according to the current Cnyes quote page. The stock is down roughly half from its 52-week high of TWD 103, and that is the setup. The market has stopped valuing it as a high-margin thermal beneficiary and has gone back to treating it like a small PC peripheral supplier with fading orders.

The bear case is visible, which is exactly why the price is interesting. Q1 2026 was ugly: revenue fell to TWD 325 million, gross margin compressed to 24.4%, operating profit turned slightly negative, and EPS fell to TWD 0.75 from TWD 1.71 a year earlier. April revenue then dropped 52.8% year over year to TWD 90.4 million, taking January to April revenue down 33.8%. Management blamed delayed pull-in from gaming-related customers, with AI demand absorbing chip, DRAM, and GPU supply and crowding out the broader gaming channel, according to MoneyDJ and Cnyes. That is a real risk, but it is also the variant perception. If this is demand destruction, the stock is merely statistically cheap. If it is a supply and timing air pocket ahead of another high-end GPU, AI PC, and server cooling cycle, the market is giving investors a recovery call option at book value.

The 2025 numbers show what the business can earn when the mix and utilization are right. Sun Max reported 2025 revenue of TWD 1.753 billion, up 38.8%, gross margin of 33.7%, operating margin of 14.7%, net income of TWD 210 million, and EPS of TWD 5.37, with a proposed TWD 4.00 cash dividend. At today’s TWD 52.50 price, that is 9.8x 2025 EPS and a 7.6% prospective dividend yield if the proposed payout is distributed. The company’s 2025 Q3 investor deck also shows that Q1 to Q3 2025 revenue rose 38.8%, gross profit rose 85.0%, operating profit rose 262%, and EPS rose 84%. That was not a one-quarter accounting fluke. It was operating leverage in a small, vertically integrated fan maker with higher-value products.

The key question is whether 2025 was peak gaming or the first proof point of a higher-spec thermal cycle. I lean toward the latter, with volatility. Sun Max still has a PC-heavy mix, so it should not be valued like Delta Electronics or a liquid-cooling pure play. But the company’s product map includes GPU and PC fans, server and netcom, industrial control, automotive, and high-reliability thermal applications. Management’s own deck emphasizes high wind pressure, speed, reliability, long life, and low noise, plus vertical integration across mold, plastic injection, SMT, motor, winding, testing, and assembly. That matters because modern high-end GPUs, AI PCs, compact workstations, edge servers, and dense electronics are not buying generic low-end fans. They are buying qualified thermal parts where noise, reliability, failure rate, and mechanical tolerances matter.

The “AI” angle should be framed correctly. Sun Max is not a core NVIDIA rack-scale liquid-cooling winner. It is a lower-tier thermal component supplier with exposure to the physical side effects of higher compute density. Its deck notes that data centers, servers, AI, HPC, automotive, netcom, and electronics miniaturization all increase demand for cooling, and that even as architectures move toward hybrid water plus air solutions, air cooling remains important in servers. That is enough for optionality. The market does not need Sun Max to become a dominant AI server vendor. It only needs gaming to normalize, server and netcom to become a larger mix, and automotive or Thailand capacity to add a second growth leg.

The Thailand investment is important for that reason. In May 2026, Sun Max approved a USD 5 million capital injection into Power Logic Tech Thailand, funded with internal resources, after prior spending on land, plant, expansion, renovations, and equipment. That is not a financial-engineering story. It is a capacity and geography story. For a small KY-structured company with China production exposure, Thailand gives customers a non-China manufacturing route, supports automotive qualification, and gives the company a more credible answer to tariff and supply-chain diversification demands. It will absorb cash before it contributes earnings, but it also makes the business less of a single-cycle China/Taiwan PC fan assembler.

The balance sheet is good enough to wait, but not good enough to be lazy. Q1 assets were TWD 3.53 billion, liabilities TWD 1.37 billion, and equity attributable to the parent was TWD 2.15 billion. Goodinfo shows Q1 cash around TWD 1.07 billion, but this should not be treated as pure surplus because the company has debt, capex needs, a dividend commitment, and Thailand funding. The better margin of safety is book value plus demonstrated earnings power, not just cash. At 1.0x book, the market is assigning almost no franchise premium to a business that earned mid-teens operating margins in 2025.

The retail setup is also favorable for torque. Goodinfo shows domestic individuals holding roughly 75% of shares, foreign investors about 22%, and average 2026 daily volume of only a few hundred thousand shares. This is not a crowded institutional AI thermal trade. It is a small, volatile, locally traded component supplier that retail chased when revenues were strong and abandoned when Q1 revenue rolled over. That ownership structure can hurt on the way down, but it can also produce violent repricing if monthly revenue turns up in Q3 or Q4.

My base case is that 2026 is a trough reset, not a structural break. I would model FY2026 revenue of TWD 1.35 to 1.50 billion, down from 2025, with gross margin recovering from Q1 but not returning to the 2025 peak, yielding EPS around TWD 3.50 to 4.25. That is not optically screaming cheap on current-year trough earnings. The upside comes from 2027, where a normal GPU/gaming pull-in cycle, AI PC upgrade activity, early Thailand contribution, and incremental server/netcom/auto mix can bring revenue back toward TWD 1.8 to 2.1 billion and EPS back to TWD 5.75 to 7.00. Put 13x on TWD 6.25 of recoverable earnings and the stock is worth roughly TWD 80 before giving much credit for the dividend stream.

The bull case is not fantasy, but it requires mix proof. If 2025 was not peak but rather the first year of a high-spec thermal cycle, and if Sun Max’s server, netcom, automotive, and higher-end GPU products expand from small optionality to visible revenue, gross margin can move back into the low-to-mid 30s. On TWD 2.2 billion of revenue, 13% to 15% net margin would produce about TWD 285 to 330 million of net income, or roughly TWD 7.00 to 8.00 EPS on the current share base. A small profitable thermal supplier with non-China capacity, dividend discipline, and AI/HPC adjacency could trade at 14x to 16x that number. That gets to TWD 105 to 130, without needing heroic peer multiples.

The downside is that the market is right and 2025 was a short GPU restocking spike. If Q2 and Q3 monthly revenue stay near April’s depressed level, operating deleverage will matter quickly. Q1 already showed that gross margin can fall ten percentage points year over year and operating income can disappear. The other warning sign is non-operating income. Q1 net profit was positive only because non-operating items offset a small operating loss. A stock cannot be underwritten on other income if the core fan business is shrinking. The thesis breaks if revenue does not recover by late 2026, if Thailand spending outruns new orders, or if gross margin remains in the low-to-mid 20s.

Still, the current price is attractive because investors are being paid to take that timing risk. At TWD 52.50, the stock is at book value, below its own average valuation range, below its 2025 earnings power, offering a high proposed dividend yield, and priced as if the weak Q1 is the new normal. I do not need to believe Sun Max is a dominant AI server supplier. I only need to believe it remains a competent high-end cooling supplier whose 2025 margin structure can partially return after a supply-chain digestion period.

Bear target: TWD 38, down 28% from TWD 52.50. This assumes April-like weakness persists, FY2026 EPS falls toward TWD 2.00, the market values the company at roughly 0.7x book, and Thailand spending is treated as cash burn rather than growth investment.

Base target: TWD 82, up 56%. This assumes the current pull-in delay normalizes, FY2027 EPS recovers to about TWD 6.00, and the market assigns 13x earnings plus credit for the dividend and book-value support.

Bull target: TWD 125, up 138%. This assumes server/netcom/auto and high-end GPU products lift revenue above the prior peak, EPS reaches TWD 8.00, and the stock rerates to roughly 15x as a small but credible high-performance thermal supplier rather than a fading PC fan name.
